$schema: Security patterns configuration for Claude Code hooks
$description: Zero-dependency, self-documenting security rules. Each entry includes a 'reason' field explaining why the pattern is blocked.

bashPatterns.reason: Prevents recursive deletion starting from root, home, or $HOME directory
Blocks rm with both recursive and force flags (rf order) to prevent accidental mass deletion
Blocks rm with both force and recursive flags (fr order) to prevent accidental mass deletion
Prevents removal of root or home directories via rmdir
Blocks shred which irrecoverably destroys file data by overwriting
Blocks find with -delete action which can silently remove large numbers of files
Blocks find piping results to rm, which can cause mass file deletion
Blocks find piping results to shred for irrecoverable mass file destruction
Prevents force-pushing to main/master which rewrites shared history and can destroy team work
Prevents force-push shorthand to main/master which rewrites shared history
Blocks git clean -f which permanently removes untracked files without confirmation
Blocks git reset --hard which discards all uncommitted changes irrecoverably
Blocks filesystem creation commands which destroy all data on the target device
Blocks dd writing directly to disk devices which destroys partition tables and data
Blocks wipefs which erases filesystem signatures from devices
Blocks fdisk partition manipulation which can destroy disk layout
Blocks parted partition editor which can destroy disk layout and data
Blocks setting world-readable/writable/executable permissions on root paths, a severe security risk
Blocks removing all permissions on root paths which can brick the system
Blocks recursive ownership change on root paths which can break system file permissions
Blocks the classic bash fork bomb pattern :(){ :|:& };: which crashes the system via process exhaustion
Blocks dot-variant fork bomb pattern which crashes the system via process exhaustion
Blocks curl POST requests that reference shell variables, indicating potential data exfiltration
Blocks wget POST requests which could exfiltrate data to external servers
Blocks netcat listen mode which can open unauthorized network backdoors
Blocks unsetting critical environment variables which breaks shell functionality
Blocks setting PATH to empty string which makes all commands unfindable
Blocks redirecting HOME to /dev/null which breaks all home-directory-relative operations
Blocks global npm installs which modify system-wide packages outside project scope
Blocks pip system-wide installs which can conflict with OS package manager
Blocks system-wide gem installs which modify shared Ruby environment
Blocks pruning all Docker resources including images, which can destroy development environments
Blocks force-removing all running containers via command substitution
Blocks force-removing all Docker images via command substitution
Blocks DROP DATABASE which permanently destroys an entire database and all its data
Blocks DROP TABLE which permanently destroys a table and all its data
Blocks TRUNCATE TABLE which irrecoverably deletes all rows from a table
Blocks DELETE FROM without a WHERE clause which deletes all rows in a table
Blocks force-deleting S3 buckets which permanently destroys all stored objects
Blocks EC2 instance termination which permanently destroys running infrastructure
Blocks GCP delete operations which can destroy cloud resources
Blocks terraform destroy which tears down all managed infrastructure
Blocks overwriting the default SSH private key which can lock out remote access
Blocks removing all SSH keys from the agent which disrupts authenticated connections
Blocks removing the entire crontab which destroys all scheduled jobs
Blocks interactive crontab editing which could introduce unauthorized scheduled tasks
Blocks redirecting infinite 'yes' output to a file which fills disk to capacity
Blocks redirecting infinite random data to a file which fills disk to capacity
Blocks writing gigabytes of random data to a file which fills disk space

zeroAccessPaths.reason: Environment files contain secrets, API keys, and credentials that must never be read or modified
SSH directory contains private keys and authorized_keys; access could compromise remote authentication
AWS credentials directory; access could enable unauthorized cloud resource manipulation
GCP credentials directory; access could enable unauthorized Google Cloud operations
PEM files contain private keys or certificates used for TLS/SSH authentication
Key files contain private cryptographic keys for authentication and encryption
PKCS#12 files contain bundled private keys and certificates
PFX files contain bundled private keys and certificates (Windows format)
Terraform state files contain sensitive infrastructure details and sometimes credentials
Terraform state backups contain the same sensitive infrastructure data as tfstate files
RSA private key file; exposure compromises all systems authenticated with this key
Ed25519 private key file; exposure compromises all systems authenticated with this key
Common credential store file containing API keys, tokens, or service account data
GCP service account key files; exposure grants full access to associated cloud permissions

readOnlyPaths.reason: System configuration directory; modifications can break OS-level services and security
Dependency directory managed by npm/yarn; manual edits are overwritten and cause inconsistencies
Build output directory; should only be modified by the build toolchain, not manual edits
Build output directory; should only be modified by the build toolchain, not manual edits
Lock files are auto-generated by package managers; manual edits cause dependency resolution failures
Minified JavaScript files are build artifacts; edits are unreadable and overwritten on rebuild
Minified CSS files are build artifacts; edits are unreadable and overwritten on rebuild
Vendored dependencies directory; manual edits bypass dependency management and break updates
Git object store; corruption here destroys repository history permanently
Git references; corruption here can orphan branches and lose commit pointers
npm lock file; manual edits cause dependency tree inconsistencies and install failures
Yarn lock file; manual edits cause dependency tree inconsistencies and install failures
Rust lock file; manual edits cause dependency resolution failures in cargo builds
Poetry lock file; manual edits cause dependency resolution failures in Python projects
